title: Quantization for NLP
concept: Quantization compresses model weights (e.g. FP16 down to INT8 or INT4), reducing GPU memory usage by 2-4x with minimal accuracy loss.

exercises.prompt: What is the primary benefit of applying quantization to an NLP model?
exercises.explain: Quantization shrinks the physical size of the model's weights in memory, allowing them to fit into smaller GPUs.

cards.title: Shrinking Giant Brains
cards.body: Modern NLP models are massive, requiring giant, expensive supercomputers just to run. But what if we could shrink them to run on your phone or laptop without making them dumb? Enter the magic of quantization!

cards.title: What is Quantization?
cards.body: Quantization is a model compression technique that converts weight values from high-precision formats (like 16-bit decimals) into lower-precision formats (like 8-bit or 4-bit integers). This makes AI models lighter and faster without losing their smarts.

cards.title: The Photo Resize
cards.body: Think of it like saving a high-resolution photo as a smaller JPEG. You lose a tiny bit of pixel-perfect detail, but the image loads instantly and looks virtually identical to the human eye. Quantization does the exact same thing to AI weights!

cards.title: Quantization in Action
cards.body: If a raw model requires 32 GB of GPU VRAM to run, applying 8-bit quantization can compress its size down to around 16 GB, and 4-bit quantization can shrink it to just 8 GB. Now you can run it on a standard laptop GPU instead of an expensive data center rig!

cards.title: Myth: Lower Precision = Broken AI
cards.body: Many believe that converting 16-bit decimals to 8-bit integers will completely ruin a model's language skills. In reality, modern quantization algorithms are so smart that the model's accuracy drops by only a tiny, barely noticeable fraction!

cards.title: Memory Footprint
cards.body: Comparing how weights are represented and stored before and after applying quantization.

left.text: Uses high-precision decimal numbers, requiring massive GPU memory.

right.text: Uses lower-precision integers, saving up to 4x GPU memory space.

exercises.prompt: How does quantization reduce a model's size?

options.text: By adding more layers to the model
By converting weights from high-precision decimals to lower-precision integers
By converting text into raw binary code
By training the model on twice as much data

exercises.explain: Quantization typically compresses weights from 16-bit floating points (FP16) down to 8-bit (INT8) or 4-bit (INT4) integers, resulting in 2-4x memory savings.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Quantizing a model can reduce its memory footprint by 2x to 4x with very little loss in quality.
exercises.explain: Quantization is highly popular because it shrinks memory usage by 2x to 4x while keeping model accuracy almost identical.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to describe how quantization saves space.
exercises.sentence: Quantization works by reducing the numerical [blank] of the model's weights.
exercises.explain: Quantization reduces the precision of model weights (like going from FP16 to INT8) to save memory.

exercises.prompt: Which of these representations represents the raw, unquantized high-precision format that consumes the most memory?
exercises.explain: Using floating-point numbers with 32 bits (FP32) is high-precision and unquantized, which requires the most memory. INT8 and INT4 are quantized formats.

exercises.prompt: Imagine you are deploying a large language model on a server with limited memory. Why would you apply quantization?

options.text: It guarantees the model will run 100% faster with zero accuracy loss.
It shrinks weights to fit models into smaller GPU memory with minimal accuracy loss.
It generates extra synthetic training data to make the model smarter.
It deletes half of the model's layers to simplify the network structure.

exercises.explain: Quantization saves massive amounts of GPU memory (often 2x to 4x) by shrinking the precision of weights, allowing huge models to run on cheaper or smaller hardware with only a tiny, negligible drop in accuracy.
